content:
# OPTIMIZATION OF VERTICAL PLACEMENT OF HORIZONTAL WELLS IN MATURE CARBONATE RESERVOIRS

**Summary**: The thesis investigates the optimal vertical placement and lateral length of horizontal wells in a mature carbonate reservoir to maximize oil recovery from an under-depleted upper zone (Zone-1). Using a full-field simulation model, it evaluates how these parameters impact cumulative production, water cut, and well life.

---

## Research Problem

The study addresses the challenge of recovering bypassed oil in a mature carbonate reservoir where the top layer (Zone-1) remains largely un-swept due to high heterogeneity and poor rock quality compared to lower zones. The specific problem is determining the optimal vertical placement and lateral length for horizontal wells to maximize recovery while minimizing water production.

## Objectives

- Utilize a full-field simulation model to optimize the vertical placement of horizontal wells.
- Determine the optimal length of reservoir contact for horizontal wells in thin oil zones.
- Evaluate the impact of varying well parameters on production from un-swept zones at the top of a thick, partially swept carbonate reservoir.
- Assess the impact of vertical placement on well performance in Zone-1.
- Evaluate the effect of reservoir contact length on cumulative oil production and water cut.
- Identify the optimal configuration for side-tracked horizontal wells to maximize recovery from under-depleted zones.

## Methods

The study utilizes a full-field simulation model to perform history matching of existing wells (Well-A, B, C, D) and their offsets. The model incorporates petrophysical data and well logs (C/O and resistivity). Prediction runs are conducted by varying two main parameters: vertical placement (top layer L-1, middle layer L-2, bottom layer L-3) and reservoir contact length (2,000 ft, 4,000 ft, 6,000 ft, and 8,000 ft). Performance is evaluated based on oil rate, water cut, and cumulative production.

## Data and Materials

The study uses data from four candidate wells (Well-A, Well-B, Well-C, Well-D) and their respective offset wells. Data includes historical production records (oil rates, pressures, water cuts), petrophysical logs (Carbon/Oxygen and resistivity), and a full-field simulation model.

## Key Findings

- The study identified that the upper most zones of large carbonate reservoirs (Zone-1) remain largely un-swept even in mature areas due to lower rock quality and limited thickness. ([[2014-al-julaih|Al-Julaih, 2014, p. 36]])
- Placing horizontal wells in the target zone results in a significant added recovery, with potential for higher production rates compared to vertical wells. ([[2014-al-julaih|Al-Julaih, 2014, p. 14]])
- Recovery can be maximized by placing the laterals in the top layer of the reservoir. ([[2014-al-julaih|Al-Julaih, 2014, p. 14]])
- The presence of natural fractures plays a major role in well performance within these zones. ([[2014-al-julaih|Al-Julaih, 2014, p. 14]])
- Well-D's target zone (Zone-1) has thickness ranging from 6 to 15 feet and a porosity between 18% and 25%. ([[2014-al-julaih|Al-Julaih, 2014, p. 38]])
- A porosity-thickness (ΣΦh) map was used as the primary basis for selecting Zone-1 sidetrack and new well candidates. ([[2014-al-julaih|Al-Julaih, 2014, p. 34]])
- The study showed that a horizontal well with 2500 ft of reservoir contact can produce twice as much as a vertical well in the same layer, while a 4000 ft contact can produce four times more. ([[2014-al-julaih|Al-Julaih, 2014, p. 26]])
- Placement of horizontal laterals in the top layer (L-1) consistently resulted in higher cumulative oil production and lower water cut compared to L-2 or L-3 across all four wells. ([[2014-al-julaih|Al-Julaih, 2014, p. 78]])
- Well-B placed in the bottom layer (L-3) was predicted to fail within 10 years due to excessive water production from nearby depleted zones. ([[2014-al-julaih|Al-Julaih, 2014, p. 76]])
- For Well-A, a lateral length of 6,000 ft yielded the highest cumulative oil (10.72 MMSTB) and lowest water cut compared to other lengths. ([[2014-al-julaih|Al-Julaih, 2014, p. 80]])
- In Well-A, an 8,000 ft lateral length resulted in poor performance due to the intersection of a major conductive natural fracture causing excessive water production. ([[2014-al-julaih|Al-Julaih, 2014, p. 79]])
- For Wells B, C, and D, increasing the reservoir contact from 4,000 ft to 6,000 ft resulted in significant improvements in cumulative oil production (18%, 30%, and 20% respectively). ([[2014-al-julaih|Al-Julaih, 2014, p. 73]])
- Increasing length from 6,000 ft to 8,000 ft for Wells B, C, and D resulted in only minor improvements (approx. 4%) due to lower rock quality in the final 2,000 ft. ([[2014-al-julaih|Al-Julaih, 2014, p. 83]])
- Well-A produced between 15-20 ft of remaining oil column (ROC) at the top for various wells, while Zone-1 was confirmed as un-swept via C/O and resistivity logs. ([[2014-al-julaih|Al-Julaih, 2014, p. 41]])
- Well-B's production in L-1 is predicted to reach a water cut of 79.5% by 2035, while L-2 reaches 84.4% and L-3 results in well failure. ([[2014-al-julaih|Al-Julaih, 2014, p. 76]])

## Limitations

Not stated in source

## Recommendations and Future Work

It is recommended to place wells in the uppermost layer (Zone-1) provided it has reasonable rock quality. Well length should be maximized while avoiding natural fractures and areas of low rock quality.
